//: PRD-027 D-5 / AC-5 — the committed recall-eval GOLDEN SET. Hand-curated (query -> expected memory) pairs scoring whether the recall engine surfaces the right memory and how high. Read eval/README.md for how this runs and how to grow it.
//schema: Each pair: { key, memoryText, query, lexicalMiss, relevance? }. `key` is a stable slug; the harness uniquifies it per-run (key + RUN_ID) so a live seed reads ONLY this run's rows. `memoryText` is STORED as a memory; `query` is run through recall; a pair PASSES when the stored memory is surfaced (its id ranks in the top-k). `lexicalMiss: true` means the query shares NO surface token with memoryText — only the <#> semantic arm can bridge it (these pairs exercise the PRD-025 lift and MUST drop out under lexical-only recall). `relevance` is the graded gain for nDCG (default 1 = binary).
//relevance: PRD-047f f-AC-1 — GRADED relevance on a 1–3 scale: 3 = the query is a near-restatement of its target, the exact + unambiguous answer (the keyword-precise lexical pairs); 2 = a strongly-related paraphrase the <#> semantic arm bridges, clearly the right answer phrased differently (the concrete lexical-miss pairs); 1 = a tangential / abstract conceptual gesture where the target is the intended answer but the query could plausibly map to several principles (the broadest semantic-miss pairs, kept binary). Grades are conservative + defensible: every pair still has exactly ONE seeded target, so recall@5 / MRR are unchanged; the grade only shapes nDCG's position-discounted gain. Re-grade only from real dogfood evidence.
//no-secret: PURELY SYNTHETIC engineering scenarios. NO tokens, NO API keys, NO emails, NO real hostnames/IPs, NO PII. `npm run audit:sql` does not scan eval/, so the no-secret bar here is the committed grep proof in the Wave-2b report + the security pass (AC-7). Keep it clean when you grow it.
//grow: Grow from REAL dogfood misses (D-5): when a live recall surfaces the wrong memory or misses the right one, distill the (query -> expected) into a new pair here, tag lexicalMiss honestly, and re-run the eval. The lexical-miss pairs are the load-bearing ones — never let the set drift to all-surface-overlap or the semantic lift goes unmeasured.
